titleStmt.title: Letter from Patrick O'Donoghue to the Acting Secretary, Irish Government, June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Patrick O'Donoghue

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Patrick O'Donoghue to the 'acting secretary, Irish Government'. At this brief period there had been no official Chief Secretary for Ireland due to the resignation of Augustine Birrell (1850-1933) following the failure of his administration to deal with the 1916 Rising. O'Donoghue enquires about the whereabouts of Patrick Joseph Ryan (b. 1881), a town councillor who was arrested in connection with the 1916 Easter Rising. It goes on to enquire if Ryan is imprisoned with a sentence or simply interned in a camp.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5612/9461/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Reply that he should
address the Provost
Marshal, Richmond
Bks. Dublin WMC 5/6 125 Queens Road Cheetham Manchester The acting Sec. Irish Government Dublin Dear Sir would you be good enough to let
me know where Mr Patrick Ryan (Town Councillor)
of Ballaghdereen, Ireland,
who was arrested in
connection with the recent
rebellion, is, and if
he is in prison or only
a prisoner of war
only in an internment camp. By a prisoner I mean suffering a term of imprisonment. Thanking
you in anticipation.
Yours etc
P O'Donoghue
